issue_title: Add a more generic `handle_failure` option to subsume `logger`
issue_body:
Logging at WARN level isn't ideal, even if you can supply your own logger you still can't (easily) change the log level that messages go to.

A more generic way to implement this would be to pass in not a logger, but a log function that gets called with information about the failure, this gives you more flexibility in how you format your log message, what level you log it at, in fact, it also gives you the flexibility to do anything else following a failure. (e.g. re-establish a connection before the next retry attempt)

Add a `handle_failure` argument which takes an optional function that gets called following a failure.

Open question as to whether it should be called before the sleep (better suited to logging) or after the sleep (better suited to recycling resources closer to when they will be used e.g. re-opening an FTP connection before a long sleep only to have it timeout from inactivity during the sleep won't work well.)

Thoughts?

issue_title: Pylama does not handle pydocstyle's ignore-decorators option.
issue_body:
In version 2.0.0 of pydocstyle they added support to ignore methods based on their decorator, but this is not supported by Pylama. Currently in pylama_pydocstyle.py, Pylama passes a hard-coded None for the ignore_decorators parameter into pydocstyle's check_source method.

It would be great to be able to add ignore_decorators options to my config file and have pylama pick them up.

issue_title: multiple IPv6 extension headers of the same type aren't handled
issue_body:
_From [nux...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/106810974862880856166/) on September 12, 2014 12:41:21_

dpkt stores ipv6 extension headers in a way that only allows it to store one extension header of each type.

While most of the headers may only appear once, the destination options header is allowed to appear twice and dpkt will only keep the 2nd copy when parsing a packet. In general it would be nice if invalid packets containing multiple instances of any of the options was parsed too.

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=133_

issue_title: Create timing tests
issue_body:
As discussed in #15.

- We should implement some kind of timing tests.
  - How long does it to parse, create, build a `Packet`?
  - How long does it to create a response packet to match a teach-in?
- These tests could be then replicated on low-end hardware like the Raspberry Pi, to see if we can stick to the time constraints set by the specification.
  - If not, optimise, optimise, optimise... Though this should be done anyways, I know at least a few places to look :stuck_out_tongue_closed_eyes:

Here's a screen dump of the debugger state while on the last line of `_will_be`:

`weatherutils.status_is` would return true here.

Side note: Looking at the `closest_weather` object  in the sidebar, I notice that `_sunrise_time` and `_sunset_time` are both `0`. Could that be related to the issue?

issue_title: The DSDL plugin "mandatory" and "when" priorities are not compatible with RFC 7950 section 8.1
issue_body:
@llhotka 
RFC 7950 section 8.1 mentioned：
o  The "mandatory" constraint is enforced for leafs and choices,  unless the node or any of its ancestors has a "when" condition or   "if-feature" expression that evaluates to "false".

That's mean, if "when" condition is there, should validate the "when " condition firstly, if "when" condition  evaluates as "false", the mandatory should not be validated. 

But, when use YANG2DSDL pluglin to validate the data,  it check "mandatory" always no matter whether  "when" condition it there,  please confirm whether it is a issue, thanks.

issue_title: Strings are not quoted for Xilinx ISE/xst "-generics" option
issue_body:
Hi,
I encountered a problem when using the [parameters] section of fusesoc together with ISE:

fussoc parameters of type "file" or "str"  passed to the -generics option of xst  must be quoted with ".

The generated tcl file should for example look like:
`project set "Generics, Parameters" "RamFileName=\"compiled_code/monitor.hex\"" -process "Synthesize - XST"`
Currently the \" are missing, as temporary workaround I have added them to the parameter itself
`[parameter RamFileName]
datatype=file
default=\"compiled_code/monitor.hex\"
description=Initial boot RAM contents (in hex)
paramtype=vlogparam
scope=public`
But this will not work with isim which expects the parameter without quotes :-(

In addition I'm not sure about expected semantics of "file" parameters:
I would assume that the path should be expanded to a path relative to the build root when passed to the tools, like it is done for the project files.

issue_title: Install Error :common.h:111:20: fatal error: Python.h: No such file or directory
dlib cross-platform boilerplate with web-camera (Qt based)
issue_body:
Hi all,

Hope you are all well !

Is it possible to create a cross-platform boilerplate for testing examples, maybe based on qt5 for limited computational resource, embedded systems and IoT devices ?

**Why ?**
Easy to test and boostrap, so we can improve performances more easily.

**How ?**
**Potential base #1**
I would suggest to use maybe a project like [gatherer](https://github.com/headupinclouds/gatherer) as a boilerplate base; it is a cross platform gpgpu opengl (es) shader pipeline framework: ogles_gpgpu + qt (UI). For anything related to vision, web camera, It would provide a demo for linux, ios, android and windows.

Also, it uses [hunter](https://github.com/ruslo/hunter), a CMake-driven cross-platform package manager for C++. Linux, Mac, Windows, iOS, Android, Raspberry Pi, and Polly, a toolcahain helper, 

They already have integrated DLIB [[here](https://github.com/ruslo/hunter/blob/master/cmake/projects/dlib/hunter.cmake#L79-L84)], or opencv for ios, android, desktop, windows ([link](https://github.com/ruslo/hunter/blob/master/cmake/projects/OpenCV/hunter.cmake#L227-L262)).

It is really convenient for caching,  customizing some packages build. For eg, ([dlib shared](https://github.com/ruslo/hunter/tree/master/examples/dlib), [dlib static](https://github.com/ruslo/hunter/tree/master/examples/dlib-staticLib))

**Potential base #2**
Or your have this Vuforia employee project, [Yarrar](https://github.com/ndob/yarrar), stopped because he is working full-time now (ref [discussion](https://github.com/ndob/yarrar/issues/1)) , [examples](https://github.com/ndob/yarrar/tree/master/example) (iOS,Android,Desktop and Unity) 

**Potential base #3 **
Mixing both above, I can do that.

Nb. I saw tiny-dnn using these helpers for iOS; https://github.com/tiny-dnn/tiny-dnn/tree/master/examples/ios

**Questions:**
- Is there any simple example with MMOD, or the normal facial landmark detector for iOS/Android available ? (would be funny to test this repo, @grisevg,  with [facial landmarks for sheep](https://github.com/grisevg/TIFfacealignment), it made me laugh so much :-) )
- Are NEON instructions able to improve performances for MMOD (eg.[ dlib for arm](https://github.com/jbrjake/dlib_for_arm)) ?
- Shall I start a new issue ? I just commented as it was related to the fact to test the dog hipsterizer on iOS and to bundle a default client boilerplate for dlib cpu or mobile devices.

Hope I was clear enough about this suggestion for dlib, meanwhile I wish you all an awesome day !

Nb. it was inspired/related to a previous issue: https://github.com/davisking/dlib/issues/284

Cheers,
Richard

I then pass a list of these `ObjectIdentity`'s to a `pysnmp.entity.rfc3413.oneliner.cmdgen.CommandGenerator.getCmd` with the kwarg `lookupMib` set to False.
However, this code path still seems to trigger some MIB resolution to resolve names, which is taking a big performance hit.

Is there some simple way to get get an OID without engaging any kind of MIB loading or resolution?

issue_title: Implement "tabular" data without MIB information
issue_body:
It is possible to get something very similar to tables, even without MIBs. It is doable to return a list of dicts, where each list item represents a row in the table, and the dict contains the colums where the key equals the column ID from the OID.

Why not a list of lists?

Some manufaturers have "sparse" tables. This introduces an ambiguity when using lists as columns: If an index is set to `None`, does that mean the element was missing, or does it mean it was explicitly reported as `Null`.

Using dicts removes this ambiguity.

issue_title: Obtain libraries needed for static linking (.e.,g --static)
issue_body:
@matze, would you be open if I expose an option to `.parse()` and `.libs()` for obtaining the libraries for static linking?

From `man pkg-config(1)`:

```
    --static
              Output libraries suitable for static linking.  That means including any private libraries in the output.  This relies on proper tagging in the .pc files, else a too large number of libraries will ordinarily be output.
```

I was thinking of something like `.parse(..., static=True)` and `.libs(..., static=True)`, with the default value being either `None` or `False`.  I'm open to your suggestions and am happy implement the change with a test case.

issue_title: Provide IQ API with strong ordering guarantees
issue_body:
The current IQ API has a main shortcoming: due to its inherent focus on coroutines, the strong ordering guarantees of the XMPP XML Stream get lost a bit. For example, when registering IQ handlers for two different IQs, we cannot say for sure which IQ handler executes first when the two IQs arrive in the same chunk of buffer handed to the XMLStream protocol.

To solve this, I propose the following API changes:

* ``register_iq_request_coro`` -> ``register_iq_request_awaitable`` and allow any function which returns an awaitable to be registered. This includes coroutine functions, but also normal functions returning a Future.

* ``send`` gets an additional "callback" argument for use with IQ get/set stanzas. Now I’m not 100% sure. Either:

    1. the callback must be a function returning an awaitable. It is executed right when the IQ result is received. The awaitable is awaited by and the result is returned from ``send``. **or**

    2. the callback must be a normal function without specific return type. It is simply executed right when the IQ result is received. It does not influence the result of ``send``.

This way, we will have a strong order guarantee, since those functions will be called right from the stanza processing loop.

  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyroute2/ipdb/route.py", line 342, in commit

CommitException: target device is not set 
```

It seems that e55eb2abb6669f0b8b19ae0bb214c1dea3269714 is the commit that introduces the issue.

issue_title: Do not discard existing and force continue
issue_body:
I'm solely using addic7ed for subliminal for my Tv shows. When too many requests error occur, it does two things:
1. Discard any results it has downloaded so far,
2. Skip the rest of the files. 

I think it shouldn't discard the files and should continue to attempt the rest of the files. Sometimes it's just a temporary issue and not being blocked, and need several retries to "fix it". Since this behaviour might not be wanted can we add a flag to force continue?

Also, I mentioned before that when 0 files successfully downloaded, we don't know if it's due to subtitles in the language requested already existed or because no subtitle exists in the search. It would be good if the program report it in greater details instead of 1 or 0. I don't think there exists a situation where it will return "10 subtitle files downloaded for Tv-show.s01e01.mkv" right?

issue_title: User as Channel inconsistency in send_message vs wait_for_message
issue_body:
For `send_message`, you are allowed to pass in a `User` for `destination` and have it interpreted as a `PrivateChannel` between that user and the client automatically. However for `wait_for_message`, you pass in a `User` for the `channel` parameter and it will hang forever - you must pass in the `PrivateChannel` to match.

It would be nice if the same interpretation of a `User` in `send_message` also applied to `wait_for_message`.

issue_title: HTTP->HTTPS Redirect Fails When Using Python2
issue_body:
I think this may have been alluded to in #14, but it appears that this script fails on sites that are configured to redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS when running under Python 2, presumably due to a limitation of the Python2 urllib2.

E.g. I have Apache setup to send temporary 302 redirects to send all HTTP traffic on my site to HTTPS. When I run the script using Python 2 I get:

```
$ python src/acme-tiny/acme_tiny.py --account-key ./account.key --csr ./request.csr --acme-dir /var/www/.well-known/acme-challenge/ > ./signed.crt
Parsing account key...
Parsing CSR...
Registering account...
Already registered!
Verifying www.andysayler.com...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "src/acme-tiny/acme_tiny.py", line 198, in <module>
    main(sys.argv[1:])
  File "src/acme-tiny/acme_tiny.py", line 194, in main
    signed_crt = get_crt(args.account_key, args.csr, args.acme_dir, log=LOGGER, CA=args.ca)
  File "src/acme-tiny/acme_tiny.py", line 123, in get_crt
    wellknown_path, wellknown_url))
ValueError: Wrote file to /srv/www/.well-known/acme-challenge/i4yT4IdRFELqbe3MK9RjKrWB_Ke0c8pESDsFFypE2IM, but couldn't download http://www.andysayler.com/.well-known/acme-challenge/i4yT4IdRFELqbe3MK9RjKrWB_Ke0c8pESDsFFypE2IM
```

Running the same command with Python 3 works correctly:

```
$ python3 src/acme-tiny/acme_tiny.py --account-key ./account.key --csr .request.csr --acme-dir /srv/www/.well-known/acme-challenge/ > ./signed.crt
Parsing account key...
Parsing CSR...
Registering account...
Already registered!
Verifying www.andysayler.com...
www.andysayler.com verified!
Verifying andysayler.com...
andysayler.com verified!
Signing certificate...
Certificate signed!
```

Since redirecting all HTTP traffic to HTTPS is likely a pretty common practice amongst LE users, and since Python2 is still fairly pervasive - this might be worth address -- although I'm not sure of the best solution short of using better HTTP libraries or adding extra redirect handling code to make up for the limitations of urllib2.

Short of an actual fix, it might be nice to document this limitation in the README.

I'm happy to take a crack at a fix, but I'm not sure it can be done without adding additional lines of code over your 200-line limit...

issue_title: Twisted interface
issue_body:
Kafka-Python claims to be "async" but this seems to just mean "uses lots of threads to put your I/O into the background" and not actually "event-driven".  It would be nice to have an interface which avoids the overhead (thread stacks, GIL switching, etc) and risks (sharing too much state, deadlocks, difficult to debug differing behavior at scale) of threads, but presents a similar interface.

I filed the ticket with the summary "twisted" because of my particular biases, but this should be done in a way which is as framework-neutral as possible, since supporting the `asyncio` stdlib module at some point in the future would probably be interesting as well, not to mention Tornado and Vanilla and so on.

Given that one would be consuming a queue, perhaps this would be a good landmark project for https://github.com/twisted/tubes which, despite its github URL, has an (almost) framework-neutral architecture and lots of good abstractions around flow control and propagating backpressure.

issue_title: Synchronous producer, mistaken timeout
issue_body:
Following the 'sync' example at http://kafka-python.readthedocs.org/en/1.0.1/usage.html, using a timeout of 30 seconds:

    future = producer.send(topic, message) 
    try:
      record_metadata = future.get(timeout=30)
    except KafkaError as e:
      print "Got kafkaerror {0}".format(e)

the issue is that the messages are sent to kafka just fine, but errors are **immediately** returned stating:

    Got kafka error Timeout after waiting for 30 secs.

Putting a producer.flush() before the try alleviates the issue, but the example implied it was unnecessary.

issue_title: Smarter handling of TemporaryDirectory disk space
issue_body:
When converting disks between formats, COT may need to use one or more temporary directories to store files. COT uses the [`tempfile`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/tempfile.html) module for this purpose. which by default creates temporary directories in, by preference:

   1. The directory named by the TMPDIR environment variable.
   2. The directory named by the TEMP environment variable.
   3. The directory named by the TMP environment variable.
   4. A platform-specific location:
        * On RiscOS, the directory named by the Wimp$ScrapDir environment variable.
        * On Windows, the directories C:\TEMP, C:\TMP, \TEMP, and \TMP, in that order.
        * On all other platforms, the directories /tmp, /var/tmp, and /usr/tmp, in that order.
    5. As a last resort, the current working directory.

When working with large files (such as RAW disk images), `/tmp` is often not large enough to contain these files, and COT fails ungracefully in this case.

COT should check the available disk space in the created temporary directory against the expected size of any file(s) it will store there and warn the user.

If an out of space error occurs, COT should recognize this and advise the user how to set their environment variables to select a different location.

issue_title: Getting some spurious output when parsing VCFs from Samtools - BCFTools call
issue_body:
Hi - I've run into an issue where reader.next() fails to iterate the next row.  As a workaround, I've tried to parse VCFs using fetch, by chromosome, stepping through coordinates one by one and catching exceptions.
This is also producing spurious output, and is much slower than record.next().

For debuggin purposes I wanted to have a look at any bad rows so I saved the row output.  Even though there are no rows for this chromosome, pyVCF seems to be barfing up a bunch of byte code 
```
Ignored 292707 bad lines: {
'chr1_GL383519v1_alt': {
0: '',
1: '\x1f\x8b\x08\x04\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\xff\x06\x00BC\x02\x00 4\xc5}ks\x1aI\xb2\xe8g\x9d_\xd1\xb1\x1b\xe7\xae\xbd\x16\xa8\xeb]%_m\x04F\x16\xa3\x1d\xeb1\x92\xc6\xb3\xfb\xe5\x12\x08\x90\xc4\x19\x04Z@\x9e\xf1\t\xfd\xf8\x9bY\xfd\xaeNZ\x8c-4\xf2\x0c\xd0\x90\xd5Y\x95\x95\xef\xca\xaa\xfe\xeb_o&\xd3\xf1\xcd|q?X\x1d|\xee\x1e}\x91m\xfe_\x7f\xfd\xeb...
```

I guess my main quesiton is, if pyVCF finds a bad line using reader.next()  do you know of a way I can try/catch exception and advance to next vcf record, without actually attempting to read it, while saving the line number in a buffer for later inspection?  Currently it throws an un-helpful iterator error.  I can catch the exception but next() stops at the bad row. 
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/usr/local/bin/pipelines/parse_vcf.py", line 313, in <module>
    record = next(indelsVCFReader)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/vcf/parser.py", line 542, in next
    line = next(self.reader)
StopIteration
```

Pickling of Region types in Python 3.6

issue_title: Add a shortcut to delete process filter
issue_body:
Add a shortcut ('F' key for example) to clear the process filter.

Change the message:

    Process filter: .*python.* (press ENTER to edit)

by:

    Process filter: .*python.* (press ENTER to edit or 'F' to clear)

When a filter is cleared, it should be saved in memory. When a user set again a filter the default field should be filled with the saved filter.

issue_title: Сделать постраничный вывод данных в гриде на стороне сервера
Abuse of `setup_requires` for the non-runtime dependency `pytest-runner`
issue_body:
The `setup.py` script has as a setup requirement a test-time one, `pytest-runner`.
According to [the docs](https://pip.readthedocs.io/en/1.4.1/cookbook.html#controlling-setup-requires), `setup_requires` is for dependencies of the setup-tools themselves.

Such requirements typically are placed in the `tests_require` argument.
If this is currently impossible due to some installation script, there is additionally the `test_suite` argument for facilitating the launching of tests.

Thank you for your time.

issue_title: Help page adds "CONFIGFILE or " for each configfile, including the final one (so always have trailing "or" + " ")
issue_body:
Repro:

```
$ cat >junktest <<EOF
import configargparse
p = configargparse.Parser(default_config_files=['woot1', 'woot2'])
p.add('--foo', help="Store some foo")
print(p.parse())
EOF

$ python junktest -h
usage: junktest [-h] [--foo FOO]

Args that start with '--' (eg. --foo) can also be set in a config file (woot1
or woot2 or ) by using .ini or .yaml-style syntax (eg. foo=value). If an arg
is specified in more than one place, then command-line values override config
file values which override defaults.

issue_title: Imports do not follow PEP8
issue_body:
[PEP8 Imports](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#imports)

> Imports should usually be on separate lines, e.g.:
> 
> Yes: import os
>      import sys
> 
> No:  import sys, os

This can cause conflicts with merging if two PR's add imports at the same time.

issue_body:
version:
QTPyLib (1.5.45)
ezIBpy (1.12.42)

algo.py line 283, self.bars = history

```python
# get history
if not self.blotter_args["dbskip"] and (self.backtest or self.preload):

    start = self.backtest_start if self.backtest else tools.backdate(self.preload)
    end = self.backtest_end if self.backtest else None

    history = self.blotter.history(
        symbols    = self.symbols,
        start      = start,
        end        = end,
        resolution = self.resolution,
        tz         = self.timezone,
        continuous = self.continuous
    )

    # history needs backfilling?
    if not self.blotter.backfilled:
        # "loan" Blotter our ibConn
        self.blotter.ibConn = self.ibConn

        # call the back fill
        self.blotter.backfill(data=history, resolution=self.resolution, start=start, end=end)

        # re-get history from db
        history = self.blotter.history(
            symbols    = self.symbols,
            start      = start,
            end        = end,
            resolution = self.resolution,
            tz         = self.timezone,
            continuous = self.continuous

        # take our ibConn back :)
        self.blotter.ibConn = None

if self.backtest:
    # initiate strategy

    # drip history

        self._tick_handler if self.resolution[-1] in ("K", "V") else self._bar_handler)

else:

issue_title: mlaunch should print error message for invalid options, rather than silently ignoring
issue_body:
Currently, `mlaunch` will silently ignore any invalid options that you attempt to pass through. For example, the following will not produce any syntax errors:

```
mlaunch init --single --carrots --apples
```

I understand that we will need to check both `mongos` and `mongod` for what is a valid option - however, it would be nice to error out for invalid syntax - otherwise a user may assume something works (e.g. setting storage engine to WiredTiger) when they actually mis-spelt it, and it did not work.

issue_title: JavaScript test file to generate example logs for cross-release testing: core
issue_body:
Create a JavaScript file with a set of queries and commands useful for comparing log parsing across different releases of MongoDB. This should exclude examples of core commands that would apply to any MongoDB deployment: inserts, updates, findAndModify, bulk writes, indexes (collection scan, index scan, ..), fast counts, count with criteria, etc.

The goal is to:
 - use [`create_mongodb_logs`](https://github.com/jorge-imperial/create_mongodb_logs) to generate test logs for all supported releases of MongoDB server
 - write a test suite invoking mtools' `util/logevent.py` to extract and compare metrics from the logs
 - identify variations in logging (eg. #409) and raise tracking issues to work out how to handle these

issue_title: [Request Feature] Timeline
issue_body:
Hey,

this is quite some awesome code you put together. I would like to propose an improvement:

Assuming the following scenario: 
I have data from three years and I would like to divide them into three parts, so in the end I have a word cloud with the upper 33% being from data from the first year, the middle 33% from the second year and the bottom 33% being words from the third year and in between the data there is a smooth transition of words.

Currently I'm doing this by creating a word cloud applying the years to three different masks (the top, middle and bottom 33%) and put it together into a full cloud, leaving visible seperation marks in the result (between the slices).

So I thought, I can code this by my own, but I cant. However I thought of three possible ways to do this:

1. Input a `list of tuples` or a `dataframe` with the left part containing a string and the right part a datetime.
2. Input as a list of `n` parts, e.g. `n=3` like above, so it looks like this `[[a, b, a, f], [g, e, a], [b, b, g, a]]`.
3. Input as normal and defining a number `n` and the input gets evenly divided into `n` parts (assuming that each time-period has the same amount of words).

I think option 2 might be the best, as option 1 and 3 might require some preprocessing first.

issue_title: Listener for sent messages
issue_body:
I'm using a PCAN-USB adaptor with a Windows 10 machine. I'd like to log all messages in either direction. For received messages I'm using a standard listener (`ASCWriter`), but I can't see a simple way of doing this for outgoing messages. 

I've seen a couple of devices (e.g. Vector) have a `receive_own_messages` flag, but not PCAN. It doesn't seem like I can set it to loopback mode on Windows, though I may well be wrong?

As a temporary solution, I've subclassed `PCanBus` and added the listeners I'm using to receive messages as an attribute. Then I've overwritten the `send()` method, copying exactly the source code but then adding a couple of lines to call the `on_message_received()` method for each listener, with the message which was just sent. Obviously this isn't a great long-term solution.

If anyone has any advice on loopback etc for the PCAN then I'm fine with that solution, otherwise is there an easier means of logging sent messages via the library?

issue_title: Document using honcho as a process manager
issue_body:
Honcho is great and it's simple to use as a library, but I had to just kinda reverse engineer what was going to to figure out how to do so. But being able to just leverage the `honcho.manager:Manager` makes it super simple to replace other subprocess managing.

Inside Sentry, we were doing this by hand, which was a bit annoying. I replaced our `sentry devserver` command to utilize `honcho` without needing to use a Procfile (since our processes are already defined by Sentry itself) and this is super seamless.

So I'm just requesting to add some documentation for this since it wasn't clear on how to do this without reverse engineering `honcho.command:command_run` and figuring it out.

For context: https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/pull/2905

Thanks. :heart:

issue_title: Rethink the meaning of ConfigItem
issue_body:
Maybe `ConfigItem` shouldn't really float around on its own with its long `path` ... instead it can be seen as a primitive thing with just a `name` and some attributes, and then the `path` actually takes shape depending on where you put the item.

Consider this:

```
class AppConfig(ConfigManager):
    class uploads:
        enabled = True
        threads = 1
        for_ = ConfigItem(name='for', help='Just a demonstration of custom name item with no default value')

config = AppConfig()
assert config.uploads.threads.type is int
assert config.uploads.enabled.type is bool
assert config.uploads.enabled.value is True
assert config.get('uploads', 'for') is NOT_SET
assert len(list(config.iter_paths())) == 3
```

Perhaps it is best to implement this as a new class, that way `ConfigItem` won't be broken idefinitely during the exploration.

issue_title: UnicodeEncodeError on warnings
issue_body:
Emitting any warning related to strings containing non-ascii literals raises `UnicodeEncodeError` in Python 2.7.x instead of just printing the warning and continue the process.

```python
import io
improt xlsxwriter

stream = io.BytesIO()
book = xlsxwriter.Workbook(stream)
>>> with book:
...     sheet = book.add_worksheet()
...     sheet.write('A1', u'http://' + u'í' * 255)
... 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 3, in <module>
  File "xlsxwriter/worksheet.py", line 63, in cell_wrapper
    return method(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "xlsxwriter/worksheet.py", line 399, in write
    return self.write_url(row, col, *args)
  File "xlsxwriter/worksheet.py", line 63, in cell_wrapper
    return method(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "xlsxwriter/worksheet.py", line 845, in write_url
    "Excel's limit for URLS" % url)
  File "/home/jsl/.virtualenvs/XlsWriter/lib/python2.7/warnings.py", line 33, in _show_warning
    file.write(formatwarning(message, category, filename, lineno, line))
  File "/home/jsl/.virtualenvs/XlsWriter/lib/python2.7/warnings.py", line 42, in formatwarning
    s =  "%s:%s: %s: %s\n" % (filename, lineno, category.__name__, message)
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 21-275: ordinal not in range(128)
```

Python version: 2.7.10
Library version: 0.7.3 ( 877c4dfac6c20006)

All warnings looks like this:

```
   # Excel limits escaped URL to 255 characters.
        if len(url) > 255:
            warn("Ignoring URL '%s' > 255 characters since it exceeds "
                 "Excel's limit for URLS" % url)
            return -3
```

The code is using native strings for all warnings which breaks with unicode strings in Python 2. Options being:

1. Force unicode  using unicode literals `u""` (breaks Python 3.2)
2. Use `from __future__ import unicode_literals` (dangerous)
3. Use conditional encoding/decoding to assure output data is in native string format

Tried (2) by inserting it  in every file along with `# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-` but severely breaks all string handling, so it does not seem to be a good solution.

A (3) approach could be implemented using type checking with an ugly compatibility function like this:

```python
def assure_unicode(string):
    if sys.version_info[0] == 2:
        if isinstance(string, unicode):
             return string.encode('utf-8')
    return string
```

issue_title: If statements coerces objects to boolean
issue_body: Best described by example: In js: ```js var Mustache = require('mustache') var body = '{{#foobar}}\n' + ' {{#link}}\n' + ' {{ text}}\n' + ' {{/link}}\n' + ' {{^link}}\n' + ' {{ . }}\n' + ' {{/link}}\n' + '{{/foobar}}'; var values = [ 'foo', 'bar', {'link': '/', 'text': 'link test'}, {'link': '/x', 'text': 'x'}, 'spam' ] console.log(Mustache.render(body, {'foobar': values})) ``` foo bar link test x spam ``` In python using pystache and chevron: ```python import pystache import chevron body = """ {{#foobar}} {{#link}} {{ text}} {{/link}} {{^link}} {{ . }} {{/link}} {{/foobar}} """ values = [ 'foo', 'bar', {'link': '/', 'text': 'link test'}, {'link': '/x', 'text': 'x'}, 'spam' ] print('pystache:') print(pystache.render(body, {'foobar': values})) print('chevron:') print(chevron.render(body, {'foobar': values})) ``` Output: ``` pystache: foo bar link test x spam chevron: True True link test x True ``` The values of the list are displayed as bools if the if statement `{{#link}}` is included. Obviously, without that the values display properly. I've no idea what the spec says about this but I think chrevon should behave the same as mustache.js.

issue_title: intersection of a line and cubicbezier
issue_body:
I found that there is an issue with intersection function. May be there is no issue but this code did something that I didn't expect. So imagine there are 2 Paths and you want find out the intersection point.

```
from svgpathtools import *

# some shapes
A = Line(start=(0+200j), end=(300+200j))
B = CubicBezier(start=(50+150j), control1=(70+200j), control2=(120+250j), end=(200+300j))

# intersection points ((T1, seg1, t1), (T2, seg2, t2))
A_intersection = Path(A).intersect(B, justonemode=True)
B_intersection = Path(B).intersect(A, justonemode=True)

disvg([A,B], nodes=[A.point(A_intersection[0][0]), B.point(B_intersection[0][0])])
```
I except 2 nodes with approximately same coordinates, but they differs. May be using fixed indices at `A_intersection[0][0]` is not the best option to figure out the T value of the path. Let's see what is in` A_intersection` and `B_intersection`:

```
>>> A_intersection
((0.33333333333333331, Line(start=200j, end=(200+200j)), 0.33333333333333331),
 (0.40000000000000002,
  CubicBezier(start=(50+150j), control1=(70+200j), control2=(120+250j), end=(200+300j)),
  0.40000000000000002))
>>> B_intersection
((0.33333333333333331,
  CubicBezier(start=(50+150j), control1=(70+200j), control2=(120+250j), end=(200+300j)),
  0.33333333333333331),
 (0.40000000000000002, Line(start=200j, end=(200+200j)), 0.40000000000000002))
```

It looks for me like if you search for intersection of `Line` and `CubicBezier`, the return values of `intersect()` on Line object have wrong positions. So you get something like  ((T2, seg1, t2), (T1, seg2, t1))

issue_title: Building from a non-working directory does not include any files into the bundle
issue_body:
We are using one repository which includes multiple lambda configurations.

```
/dummy
	config-1.yaml
	config-2.yaml
	handler.py
	requirements.txt
/foo
	config.yaml
	handler.py
	requirements.txt
```

 I created a little script to create packages for each of the functions:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import aws_lambda
import os

_cwd = os.getcwd()


def build(name, config='config.yaml'):
    aws_lambda.build(
        os.path.join(_cwd, name),
        requirements=os.path.join(_cwd, name, 'requirements.txt'),
        config_file=os.path.join(_cwd, name, config)
    )


build('dummy', 'config-1.yaml')
build('dummy', 'config-2.yaml')
build('foo')

```

The resulting packages only contain the installed requirements, but not any of the lambda handler itself.

This is caused by the [`isfile` check in `aws_lambda.build`](https://github.com/nficano/python-lambda/blob/0b02ec519d2ba0b18930284e55e621c578655b09/aws_lambda/aws_lambda.py#L330). It assumes that the current directory is the source directory since it does not join the directory and filename (as done 6 lines below).

possible fix:

```diff
--- /tmp/a	2018-08-07 11:28:35.000000000 +0200
+++ /tmp/b	2018-08-07 11:28:40.000000000 +0200
@@ -327,16 +327,17 @@
 
     files = []
     for filename in os.listdir(src):
-        if os.path.isfile(filename):
+        abs_filename = os.path.join(src, filename)
+        if os.path.isfile(abs_filename):
             if filename == '.DS_Store':
                 continue
             if filename == config_file:
                 continue
             print('Bundling: %r' % filename)
-            files.append(os.path.join(src, filename))
+            files.append(abs_filename)
         elif os.path.isdir(filename) and filename in source_directories:
             print('Bundling directory: %r' % filename)
-            files.append(os.path.join(src, filename))
+            files.append(abs_filename)
 
     # "cd" into `temp_path` directory.
     os.chdir(path_to_temp)

```

issue_title: chrF fails on short references.
issue_body:
Running the command:
```bash
./chrF.py --ref <(echo Irre.) --hyp <(echo Fantastisch.)
```
leads to this error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./chrF.py", line 135, in <module>
    main(args)

    chrf, precision, recall = f1(correct, total, total_ref, args.ngram, args.beta)

    recall += (correct[i] + smooth) / (total_ref[i] + smooth)

issue_title: In mintty some symbols used by powerline-shell are not rendered
issue_body:
I am using powerline-shell in [Cygwin](https://cygwin.com) with the [mintty](https://mintty.github.io/) terminal emulator. The problem is that none of the [patched fonts](https://github.com/powerline/fonts) contains all of the symbols used by powerline-shell. Mintty seems to have its issues with font substitution/fallback. While it does appear to substitute *some* of the symbols, it doesn't do it for all. Maybe because it doesn't find a suitable replacement in a monospace font...

Any ways, attached is a test HTML [test.html.gz](https://github.com/b-ryan/powerline-shell/files/2296137/test.html.gz) that shows some (all?) of the symbols used by powerline-shell for each of the patched fonts. As a fallback font the styling uses the [LastResort](http://www.unicode.org/policies/lastresortfont_eula.html) font to force the browser not to fall back onto an arbitrary font that provides the symbol. As can be seen, **none** of the fonts provides glyphs for `u+2446` and `u+2332` that you use for a SVN and fossil repositories, respectively. The composite for bzr also fails to render due to the lack of the combining diamond glyph `u+20DF`. Same goes for stashed state, `u+2398`. Etc. pp.

I realize none of this is the fault of powerline-shell per se. It is caused by the interaction of fonts not having the glyphs and mintty not substituting as liberally as other terminal emulators do. Hence this is more of a question. Could an alternative set of symbols be offered/configured? Just seeing tofu is not very helpful and beats the purpose of powerline-shell. I'll also follow up with the mintty developers. They are already aware that there are font substitution problems, but this being Windows the MS API's are of course behaving quite differently than the *NIX systems.

issue_title: Single page application strategy
issue_body:
Hi everyone.

I'm developing an app that it's composed by a standalone api (with django rest framework) and a single page application consuming that api. I've added PSA into my backend to add a sign in with linked in.

The strategy goes like this:
1. The user taps on a '**sign in with linked in**' button which leads to the PSA login url.
2. PSA performs the social log in. The provider redirects to the backend in both authorise and token requests.
3. At this point, I need the backend to redirect to a front-end url with the auth token of the user (as the provider does), so the single page application can use it in further requests.

The problem is that I don't see any step in which I can do the 3rd step in the PSA workflow.

Another solution could be to set the provider redirections to the front-end so it can proxy that requests to the server, using the front-end as a middleware. The problem with this one is that I didn't see where to set the provider redirection urls in the PSA configuration.

PSA is a very nice library, and could be awesome to have a description of the usage with a single page application in the documentation. I can help with this.

issue_title: ImportError: No module named 'winrandom'
issue_body:
Had problem on Windows 7 and Python 3.5.2 (64bit) when importing simplecrypt the system complained:
ImportError: No module named 'winrandom' 
Seems to be coming from Crypto.Random.random import getrandbits

Anyway as suggested [here](https://github.com/dlitz/pycrypto/issues/110) by @neRok00 following solved situation for me:

> Problem is solved by editing string in crypto\Random\OSRNG\nt.py:
> import winrandom
> to
> from . import winrandom

issue_title: Make QuerySet[slice] non-greedy

issue_title: possible security issue using non https disqus loader in the template code
issue_body:
Hi, 

I am still testing and learning Flask-Blogging v1.0.1. (much less web app coding!) I pip installed flask-blogging, and setup a test blog. I experienced a possible security problem:

While at a coffee shop, I was attacked with an injected script, some sort of ad for an antivirus... The blog page was overwritten, with "you are infected and need my antivirus..."  I was puzzled how this could happen, so i googled, it seems this could be rooted in using http to get disqus javascript code -  injecting into javascript code accessed for disqus via http instead of https. Note, this is a guess, I was not able to reproduce this problem more than once.

Suggested solution:
So, I patched my instance of the disqus.html template file - I just modified the url that is used to access the disqus, to use https, and it seems to still function, not sure if this is a complete fix. The code from disqus seems to be a little newer than what is in the disqus.html template.
My modification:
(function() {
            var dsq = document.createElement('script'); dsq.type = 'text/javascript'; dsq.async = true;
            **dsq.src = 'https://' + disqus_shortname + '.disqus.com/embed.js';**

Latest discus code ref: https://disqus.com/admin/universalcode/ 

Thanks,
Mark Menkhus

issue_title: lists, tuples, deques incorrectly converted to non-1D object arrays when all elements are ndarrays with the same dimensions
issue_body:
If we let

```
>>> a = [numpy.array([[191, 391], [391, 19]], dtype='uint8'), numpy.array([[3, 1], [3, 31]], dtype='int32')]
```

which is a two element list of ndarrays which are each 2x2.

The package has to convert the the list to a 1D object ndarray. It was doing it with``numpy.object_(a)``, which, in this case, will make a 2x2x2 object array

```
>>> numpy.object_(a).shape
(2, 2, 2)
```
When the elements of a have different shapes or some of them are not array-like, then there are no issues.

The solution is to instead do

```
>>> b = numpy.zeros(dtype='object', shape=(len(a), ))
>>> b[:] = a
>>> b.shape
(2,)
```

-        http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/dialects/postgresql.html#full-text-search
-
-    .. _SQLAlchemy-Searchable:
-        https://www.github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-searchable
-
-    ::
-
-        from sqlalchemy_utils import TSVectorType
-
-
-        class Article(Base):
-            __tablename__ = 'user'
-            id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
-            name = sa.Column(sa.String(100))
-            search_vector = sa.Column(TSVectorType)
-
-
-        # Find all articles whose name matches 'finland'
-        session.query(Article).filter(Article.search_vector.match('finland'))
-
-
-    TSVectorType also supports vector concatenation.
-
-    ::
-
-
-        class Article(Base):
-            __tablename__ = 'user'
-            id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
-            name = sa.Column(sa.String(100))
-            name_vector = sa.Column(TSVectorType)
-            content = sa.Column(sa.String)
-            content_vector = sa.Column(TSVectorType)
-
-        # Find all articles whose name or content matches 'finland'
-        session.query(Article).filter(
-            (Article.name_vector | Article.content_vector).match('finland')
-        )
-
-    You can configure TSVectorType to use a specific regconfig.
-    ::
-
-        class Article(Base):
-            __tablename__ = 'user'
-            id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
-            name = sa.Column(sa.String(100))
-            search_vector = sa.Column(
-                TSVectorType(regconfig='pg_catalog.simple')
+class TSVectorType(sa.types.UserDefinedType):
+    class comparator_factory(sa.types.TypeEngine.Comparator):
+        def match_tsquery(self, other, catalog=None):
+            from sqlalchemy_utils.expressions import tsvector_match, to_tsquery
+
+            args = []
+            if catalog:
+                args.append(catalog)
+            elif self.type.options.get('catalog'):
+                args.append(self.type.options.get('catalog'))
+            args.append(other)
+
+            return tsvector_match(
+                self.expr,
+                to_tsquery(*args)
             )

-
-    Now expression such as::
-
-
-        Article.search_vector.match('finland')
-
-
-    Would be equivalent to SQL::
-
-
-        search_vector @@ to_tsquery('pg_catalog.simgle', 'finland')
-
-    """
-    impl = TSVECTOR
-
-    class comparator_factory(TSVECTOR.Comparator):
-        def match(self, other, **kwargs):
-            if 'postgresql_regconfig' not in kwargs:
-                if 'regconfig' in self.type.options:
-                    kwargs['postgresql_regconfig'] = (
-                        self.type.options['regconfig']
-                    )
-            return TSVECTOR.Comparator.match(self, other, **kwargs)
-
-        def __or__(self, other):
-            return self.op('||')(other)
-
     def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
         """
         Initializes new TSVectorType
@@ -104,4 +28,12 @@
         """
         self.columns = args
         self.options = kwargs
-        super(TSVectorType, self).__init__()
+
+    """
+    Text search vector type for postgresql.
+    """
+    def get_col_spec(self):
+        return 'tsvector'
+
+
+ischema_names['tsvector'] = TSVectorType

issue_body:
My `sitecustomize.py` file:
```
import win_unicode_console
win_unicode_console.enable()
```
content of `foo.py`:

import sys
for line in sys.stdin:

  readline_hook.enable(use_pyreadline=use_pyreadline)
import sys
for line in sys.stdin:
    sys.stdout.write(line)
```
The warning is coming from sitecustomize.py, so I'm assuming I have to check if `sys.stdin` is coming from a pipe?  (I first tried to check if `sys.stdin.encoding is None` -- but both it and stdout.encoding are always None in sitecustomize).

This is my latest attempt:
```
import sys
if sys.stdin.isatty():
    import win_unicode_console

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

```
..but win-unicode-console is probably not involved in any of that, correct?

issue_title: Bot stops reacting after a few hours
issue_body:
1. What version of pyTelegramBotAPI are you using?
**2.3.0**
2. What OS are you using?
**Raspbian Jessie**
3. What version of python are you using?
**Python 2.7.9**

I have a simple bot that reacts to commands and I use polling like this:

```
if __name__ == "__main__":
    while True:
        try:
            log.info("start polling")
            bot.polling(none_stop=True)
        except requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout as e:
    	    log.error('ReadTimeout')
            print >> sys.stderr, str(e)
            sleep(10)
        except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError as e:
    	    log.error('ConnectionError')
            print >> sys.stderr, str(e)
            sleep(10)
        except Exception as e:
            log.error('unexpected Exception')
            print >> sys.stderr, str(e)
            sleep(10)
```

This problem occurs everytime, for example a few days ago:
I started the bot at 9 in the morning and everything worked fine. 
At 1 PM I checked again and the bot still reacted. 
At 3 PM I tried again but the bot was down.
I then took a look at the logs and saw that there were a few exceptions:

```2017-01-18 10:57:40,646 - ERROR: ReadTimeout
2017-01-18 10:58:25,722 - ERROR: ConnectionError
2017-01-18 10:59:10,798 - ERROR: ConnectionError
2017-01-18 10:59:36,575 - ERROR: ConnectionError```

And after that the usual

```2017-01-18 16:50:26,230 - DEBUG: https://api.telegram.org:443 "GET /MY_BOT_ID/getUpdates?timeout=20&offset=107698299 HTTP/1.1" 200 23
2017-01-18 16:50:26,323 - DEBUG: Starting new HTTPS connection (1): api.telegram.org```

every 20 seconds.

Obviously there are some exceptions but the bot should keep polling and it does, but then a few hours after the ReadTimeout and ConnectionsErrors it doesn’t react anymore.

I also tried ```none_stop=False``` because I thought that it might get stuck at this point and doesn't start polling again but it didn't help.

Today there were no errors at all but suddenly the bot stopped responding and I still can see the "Starting new HTTPS connection" messages.

The only thing that helps is restarting the script. Do you have any idea what I could do about that?

issue_title: decrypt_file() with output=<file with spaces> results in usage error
issue_body:
Using decrypt_file() with an output argument that includes spaces results in a usage error from gpg on Linux. Each string separated by spaces is passed as an argument to gpg rather than the string as a whole in quotes.

Attached is a quick and dirty patch that fixes the issue on Linux at least. I'm certain it's incomplete and probably buggy, but including here for reference.

[gnupg.txt](https://github.com/isislovecruft/python-gnupg/files/67395/gnupg.txt)

issue_title: Module collects files with 'test' in the name, even if they are not test files.
issue_body:
Hi. I was just looking through the codebase to familiarize myself with how it works, and I found this bug- It collects files that have `test` in the name, including something with `test` in the middle of the word:

 ``` 
╰─ touch sample/intestine.py 
╰─ ls sample 
__init__.py  intestine.py  __pycache__  settings.py  urls.py  wsgi.py
╰─ pytest --picked 
======================================================== test session starts =========================================================
platform linux -- Python 3.6.5, pytest-3.6.0, py-1.5.3, pluggy-0.6.0
rootdir: /home/misterrios/Projects/sample/sample, inifile:
plugins: picked-0.1.0
collecting 0 items                                                                                                                   
Changed test files... 1. ['sample/intestine.py']
Changed test folders... 1. ['.pytest_cache/']
collected 0 items                                                                                                                    

==================================================== no tests ran in 0.05 seconds ====================================================
```

Pytest discovers tests by checking for `test_*.py` or `*_test.py`
See: https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html#test-discovery
So, a possible solution would be to use the built-in module `pathlib` to check the filenames and the file suffix combined with `startswith` and `endswith`. See: https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html#pathlib.PurePath.name

issue_title: "Add or update a list member" doesn't compute hash
issue_body:
#24 added `member.create_or_update`, however, it expects the subscriber hash in argument instead of computing it from the email address.

This surprised me because I expected the library to deal with that sort of low level concerns.

There's a `mailchimp3.helpers.get_subscriber_hash` function to compute this hash but it doesn't appear to be called anywhere. It was added in 344398b79f92268de88689ab15cda2c3cef918e7.

It's unclear to me whether it's a design decision or an oversight to have users perform hashing themselves. Perhaps someone who knows the history of this library could help?

Three courses of actions are possible at this point:

- document that `member_id` can be computed with `get_subscriber_hash`
- document that `member_id` is an email address and compute the hash automatically
- accept both an email address and a hash; if it looks like a hash i.e. it matches `$[0-9a-f]{16}$`, use it as is; if it contains an `@` assume it's an email and hash it; if it's something else raise an exception.

I'm happy to provide a patch for either of these options.

All the other ops work just fine, but ``move`` and ``remove`` don't seem to delete anything? Is there some limitation with for example MariaDB and/or SQLAlchemy? I am using the PyMySQL driver.

```
self = <test_views.TestNewsView testMethod=test_patching_things_using_remove>

The implementation:

``PATCH /news/123``

        https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6902

The method:

            # Replace eg. /title with /Title

            mapped_patchdata.append(p)

The mapping:

    def patch_mapping(self, patch):
        """This is used to map a patch "path" or "from" to a custom value.
        Useful for when the patch path/from is not the same as the DB column name.

        Eg.
        PATCH /news/123
        [{ "op": "move", "from": "/title", "path": "/author" }]

        If the News column is "Title", having "/title" would fail to patch because the case does

        return mutable

issue_title: Block when define a Namespace
issue_body:
I used the following code, but the program blocked on socketio.define(...):
```python
class TestNamespace(SocketIONamespace):
        pass
socketio = SocketIO('127.0.0.1', 8081)
socketio.define(TestNamespace, '/test')
```

I traced the code and found that:

1. There's a default namespace after create a instance of ``SocketIO`` object.
2. The ``define()`` while blocked until all namespaces are connected.
3. In the ``_should_stop_waiting()`` function, the connection is checked by namespace's ``_connected`` property.
4. In the ``_process_packet()`` function, the namespace's _connected property is set after received the connect message.
5. But, the default namespace's ``_connected`` has never been set, so the define() is blocked.

And, I also checked the server's code:

1. The connection build on ``polling`` first, and then upgrade to ``websocket``.
2. The sever send two packet by polling when client connected: the connect packet(type=0) and message (type=4) packet.
3. But, on the client side, in ``_get_engineIO_session()`` function, only connect packet is processed. and then upgrade to ``websocket``.

Finally, I found the block is caused by forgot set ``_connected`` to the default namespace.
The fix to suggest is appending ``self._on_connect(None, self.get_namespace())`` on ``SocketIO.__init__`` function.

By the way, don't forget change ``__version__`` in your code. :-)

issue_title: Python 2 and 3 validate emails differently
issue_body:
I wrote a test wrapper around the test_pass.txt file (converted to a csv in my local copy) and saw several failures in Python 2 that passed in Python 3.  I tracked it down to an issue with Python 2's regex with utf-8 characters.

This best illustrates the issue:

Python 2:

    >>> import re
    >>> re.compile(u"[\x80-\U0010ffff]", re.DEBUG)
    in
      range (128, 56319)
      literal 57343
    <_sre.SRE_Pattern object at 0x000000000599B2D0>

Python 3:

    >>> import re
    >>> re.compile(u"[\x80-\U0010ffff]", re.DEBUG)
    IN
      RANGE (128, 1114111)
    re.compile('[\x80-\U0010ffff]', re.DEBUG)

The unicode character \U0010ffff should be 1114111, but Python 2 interprets the unicode characater as 2 characters, and so it does not recognize the full range.  The test with the local part set to u'my\uff0ename' fails because of this issue.

I have been unable to get a regex that works so far.  I suspect it's related to this:

    unichr(0x0010ffff)
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      Python Shell, prompt 114, line 1
    ValueError: unichr() arg not in range(0x10000) (narrow Python build)

issue_title: Conform to PEP 420
issue_body:
backports is a namespace package, however, it has a init.py which is conforming the rejected PEP 382. PEP 420, which is final, states that

> Namespace packages cannot contain an init.py

- PEP 420 specification: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/#specification
- PEP 382: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0382/
- Discussion where we encountered this issue: https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/22319
- Issue for `backports.ssl_match_hostname`

issue_title: make_request inside FitbitOauth2Client does not handle refresh on expired token correctly
issue_body:
Hi there,

I think there is a problem with token refreshing. On expired_token, _fitbit.exceptions.HTTPUnauthorized: Access token expired_ is still being thrown instead of handled correctly. https://github.com/orcasgit/python-fitbit/blob/master/fitbit/api.py#L82.

Inside the if condition, it checks d['errors'][0]['errorType'] == 'oauth' and ... and calls self.refresh_token() when that condition is met, but the errorType is 'expired_token' not 'oauth'. This is the JSON body:

{u'errors': [{u'errorType': u'expired_token',
              u'message': u'Access token expired: [_omitting_token_string here_]. Visit https://dev.fitbit.com/docs/oauth2 for more information on the Fitbit Web API authorization process.'}],
 u'success': False}

As a result it's never retried and we get the access token expired exception later again.

Let me know if my understanding of how this works/doesn't work is correct :)...

issue_title: Unicode chars in a query string in a websocket connection url raise UnicodeEncodeError
issue_body:
Hi, I've just got a bug related to badly formatted query string during websocket connection attempt.

# Traceback
```
[ERROR 2017-04-19 14:36:01,145 daphne.ws_protocol 82 ws_protocol: MainProcess] Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/.../virtual/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/daphne/ws_protocol.py", line 47, in onConnect
    query_string = urlencode(request.params, doseq=True).encode("ascii")
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/urllib.py", line 1368, in urlencode
    l.append(k + '=' + quote_plus(str(elt)))
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 0-23: ordinal not in range(128)
```
# Reproduce error
1. Run daphne as runserver `python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000` or ` daphne -b 0.0.0.0 -p 8000 asgi:channel_layer`
2. In Chrome console open development console
```
var socket = new WebSocket('ws://localhost:8000/?token=фффàè')
```
3. In Chrome console error:
```
WebSocket connection to 'ws://localhost:8000/?token=%D1%84%D1%84%D1%84%C3%A0%C3%A8' failed: Error during WebSocket handshake: Unexpected response code: 500
```

issue_title: Support show functions in design document module
issue_body:
Supporting show functions is part of a larger work item for design document sub-objects (#137).
- In database module, query _show endpoint: i.e. `get_show_result(show_name, ddoc_show_id, doc_id,  **kwargs)`
- In design document module, support show functions with a `shows` property accessor and management functions

issue_title: GRU not working with layout 'NTC'
issue_body:
For bugs or installation issues, please provide the following information.
The more information you provide, the more likely people will be able to help you.

## Environment info
Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04.2 LTS

Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia): Python

MXNet version: mxnet-cu80==0.10.1b20170803

If you are using python package, please provide

Python version and distribution: python 2.7.13, used with anaconda 4.2.23

## Error Message:
Please paste the full error message, including stack trace.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "simply/mx/test_rnn.py", line 14, in <module>
    output, hn = layer(input, h0)
  File "/home/huxley/dev/miniconda2/envs/mxnet/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mxnet/gluon/block.py", line 251, in __call__
    return self.forward(*args)
  File "/home/huxley/dev/miniconda2/envs/mxnet/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mxnet/gluon/rnn/rnn_layer.py", line 162, in forward
    str(info['shape']), str(state.shape)))
ValueError: Invalid recurrent state shape. Expecting (2, 2L, 100), got (2L, 8L, 100L).
```

## Minimum reproducible example
if you are using your own code, please provide a short script that reproduces the error.

## Steps to reproduce
or if you are running standard examples, please provide the commands you have run that lead to the error.

```python
import mxnet as mx

batch_size = 8
timesteps = 7
nhidden = 100
layers = 2
nin = 10

layer = mx.gluon.rnn.GRU(nhidden, layers, layout='NTC')
layer.initialize()

input = mx.nd.random_uniform(shape=(batch_size, timesteps, nin))
h0 = mx.nd.random_uniform(shape=(layers, batch_size, nhidden))
output, hn = layer(input, h0)
```
## What have you tried to solve it?

The only thing that solves it is changing the layout (and the data accordingly) to 'TNC'.

issue_title: What does each dimension the input array stands for? nrow by ncol, or ncol by nrow.
issue_body:
I searched a lot try to find some words about the dimensions of the input data array.
I'm using mxnet in R, for example, the train.array is of shape c(23,28,1, ncol(X_train)):

X_train is my training data, with each column being an sample, for example, X_train[,1] is the first sample with 644 elements. The input image has a true size of 23*28 (23 rows, 28 columns), and I vectorize the image columnwise which gives me a vector of length 644.

So am I specifying the right input array dimension? I saw all the documents about mx.symbol.Convolution is talking about shape (y,x), I'm assuming it's (nrow, ncol) fashion, and the training array is (nrow, ncol,n_ chanel, n_sample). Am I right?

Thanks!

issue_title: Fine tune tutorial urllib bug on Python 3
issue_body:
Tutorial:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/docs/faq/finetune.md

Python version and distribution:
3.6

## Steps to reproduce

1. Executing the section of code to download the dataset will fail with a urllib error unless you're using Python 2.7.x

```
urllib.urlretrieve(url, filename)
```
Yields:
```
AttributeError: module 'urllib' has no attribute 'urlretrieve'
```

This replacement for the offending code block seems to work when tested locally:

```
import os, sys

if sys.version_info[0] >= 3:
    from urllib.request import urlretrieve
else:
    from urllib import urlretrieve
    
def download(url):
    filename = url.split("/")[-1]
    if not os.path.exists(filename):
        urlretrieve(url, filename)
download('http://data.mxnet.io/data/caltech-256/caltech-256-60-train.rec')
download('http://data.mxnet.io/data/caltech-256/caltech-256-60-val.rec')
```

issue_title: Memory GPU leak with Gluon
issue_body:
I try to train a simple vgg16 on large custom database.
with this training loop:
```python
for data, label in self.base.train:
  print(label, end=" ")
  # moving datas and labels in the net's context
  data = data.as_in_context(ctx)
  label = label.as_in_context(ctx)
  # computing forward pass in the autograd environement (for gradient computation)
  with autograd.record():
    output = net(data)
    loss = scel(output, label)
    # loss back-propagation
  loss.backward()
  # trainer update
  trainer.step(data.shape[0])
  # ndarray.waitall()
```

With only this the GPU memory slowly increase until GPU crash.

If I set `MXNET_ENGINE_TYPE=NaiveEngine` memory leak disappear and if I add `ndarray.waitall()` after each batch the memory leak disappear too.

Any clue how to manage this ?

issue_title: Proposed improvements for the mxnet Scala package
issue_labels: [{'id': 206159505, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMDYxNTk1MDU=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Call%20for%20Contribution', 'name': 'Call for Contribution', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 823355562, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MjMzNTU1NjI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Feature%20request', 'name': 'Feature request', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 326513136, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzMjY1MTMxMzY=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Roadmap', 'name': 'Roadmap', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 337768593, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzMzc3Njg1OTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apache/incubator-mxnet/labels/Scala', 'name': 'Scala', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I propose the following improvements for the mxnet Scala package, mostly making it more idiomatic and user-friendly for Scala users (could be in the form of a higher-level API while retaining the current API as the lower-level API):

 - Current API is full of `kwargs: Map[String, Any]`, which is not at all idiomatic Scala. Manually rewrite them to normal Scala functions with default parameters. Without the reference of the mxnet Python API I'm really lost at how to use these layers/functions.
 - RNNs: Utilize the Scala `foldLeft`/`scanLeft`/etc operations on `Seq`s to automatically unroll the RNN computation graph. E.g. make an LSTM unit as a function of type `(LSTMState, Symbol) => LSTMState)`, so that you can just say `xs.foldLeft(lstm)` to unroll an LSTM network.
 - Revamping the BucketIO/DataBatch/etc. stuff to be more Scala-idiomatic.
 - Support for GPU on Mac OS X (I've attempted this but failed)
 - Better documentations
 - Better API for executors/gradients maps/etc.
 - [tentative] Typesafe `NDArray`s. Encode the rank/order of the tensor into the type system (What Scala is good at! ), so that an NDArray like `NDArray[Float, _3]` means that it is of rank-3 and each cell contains a `Float` (can be done with the typeclass / phantom type pattern in Scala and with shapeless (https://github.com/milessabin/shapeless)). Concatenation/slicing/etc. will all encode the ranks of the resulting NDArray/Symbol. This enhances compile-time type safety, e.g. `Embedding` layer is a function whose type is `Symbol[Int, N] => Symbol[Float, N+1]`. You cannot pass a symbol with `Float` into an embedding layer.
 - [tentative] Migrate the build system from `maven` to `sbt`.

I can first come up with a prototype of this (also for my own use in NLP research) and we can discuss.

@javelinjs @Ldpe2G @piiswrong @jermainewang

Any comments are appreciated.

```python
from __future__ import print_function
import glob
import sys
import os
import mxnet as mx

batch_size=64
ctx=mx.gpu(1)
resnet_model = get_checkpoint('resnet18',32, image_size=299, batch_size = batch_size, ctx=ctx)
fls = glob.glob('../rec/*.rec') # length(fls) = 1269
for fl in fls:
    p = predict_on_rec_v1(resnet_model, path_imgrec=fl, ctx=ctx, batch_size=batch_size)

```
Code for get_checkpoint:
```python
def get_checkpoint(prefix, epoch, ctx=mx.gpu(0), batch_size=32, image_size=299):
  height, width = image_size, image_size
  channels = 3
  sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint(prefix, epoch)
  mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=sym, context=ctx, label_names=None)
  mod.bind(for_training=False,
           data_shapes=[('data', (batch_size, channels, height, width))],
           label_shapes=mod._label_shapes)
  mod.set_params(arg_params, aux_params, allow_missing=True)
  return mod
```

```python
def predict_on_rec_v1(model=None, path_imgrec=None, p_cutoff=0.9, ctx=mx.gpu(0), batch_size=32, data_shape=(3,299,299)):
  classIdx2label = {0:'cancer',1:'normal'}
  if path_imgrec is not None:
    data_iter = mx.io.ImageRecordIter(path_imgrec=path_imgrec, data_shape=data_shape, round_batch=False, \
                  batch_size=batch_size, mean_r=123.68, mean_g=116.779, mean_b=103.939, shuffle=False) # set round_batch=False

    k_cancer, k_normal = sum(p[:,0] > p_cutoff), sum(p[:,1] > p_cutoff)

    print("cancer vs. normal, {}, {} vs. {}.".format(os.path.basename(path_imgrec), \
          k_cancer, k_normal), file=sys.stderr)
    return p
  else:
    raise ValueError("path_imgrec is None.")
  return None
```

issue_title: Passing PYTHONPATH to worker nodes in distributed training
issue_body:
## Environment info
Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04
Compiler: gcc5.x
Package used: Python
MXNet version: master
MXNet commit hash (`git rev-parse HEAD`): 9d24cfd71f14d719a67c903204d866f15e6848fc
Python version and distribution: python2.7

## Issue:
When launch.py launcher script launches a job, it doesn't respect the PYTHONPATH variable set on the master or on a worker. I was trying to use mxnet python files installed at a custom location. Despite setting PYTHONPATH variables on master and worker, it wasn't used. It still used the default python library location. I verified the version of MXNet used by printing the module.

## Steps to reproduce
Let's say we have an Ubuntu cluster. We have our python package of mxnet installed without pip. So it is in a custom location. We then put this on PYTHONPATH. Repeat this for all instances on the cluster. 
Suppose there's a simple script test.py which includes two lines.
```
import mxnet
print mxnet
```
This should give the location of mxnet python packages being imported.

Running this locally works as expected, because PYTHONPATH is set in .bashrc or exported to the bash session. 
Run this same script with launch.py, on all instances in the cluster. Each of them uses the version of mxnet installed in default site-packages location which pip uses, or fails to import if that location doesn't have mxnet.

## What have you tried to solve it?
1. I tried setting PYTHONPATH manually in both worker and master nodes.
2. I tried explicitly giving PYTHONPATH while running the launch.py script
3. I have verified that ssh.py which launches the command on nodes doesn't pass the environment variable PYTHONPATH. It only passes the variables 'OMP_NUM_THREADS', 'KMP_AFFINITY', 'LD_LIBRARY_PATH', 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID', 'AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY', 'DMLC_INTERFACE'. See code [here](https://github.com/dmlc/dmlc-core/blob/46886a6b47f660cda581e497378204ccc029a01e/tracker/dmlc_tracker/ssh.py#L26)
4. When a command is launched over ssh, it doesn't involve the shell. So it doesn't use the environment variables. I believe we should pass environment variables like PYTHONPATH explicitly as done above. Or if you think there's a better way to do this, let's discuss.

##Possible solution
[Described above in more detail, in case you skipped over to this] 
Add PYTHONPATH to the list of variables in [dmlc_tracker/ssh.py](https://github.com/dmlc/dmlc-core/blob/46886a6b47f660cda581e497378204ccc029a01e/tracker/dmlc_tracker/ssh.py#L26).

issue_title: Doesn't work with staticfiles finders, raises error when STATIC_ROOT is not set
issue_body: Pretty self-explanatory. #22, #52 and #64 are related. If you're using staticfiles with the `STATICFILES_DIRS` setting, but are not using `STATIC_ROOT`, an error is raised when trying to make absolute paths for the static files. In our case, we use staticfiles in development and use a separate storage class for S3 storage of our static files. This means `STATIC_ROOT` is never set for us, neither in development nor in production.

issue_title: Custom views for couchdb store
py_state
issue_body:
While experimenting with jsonpickle I stumbled upon a somehow disturbing behavior regarding `__getstate__` and `__setstate__`. It is also related to this issue : https://github.com/jsonpickle/jsonpickle/issues/73

Here is what I understand after a short use of jsonpickle (v0.9.2)
If the class has a `__getstate__()` method, the json is created like this:
```json
{
	"py/object": "package.module.Class",
	"py/state": {
		"members": []
	}
}
```
If not, it is created like this : 
```json
{
	"py/object": "package.module.Class",
	"members": []
}
```

When reading a json file, if there is a "py/state" key, `__setstate__()` is called, otherwise it is not called.
This behavior allows these workflow to work fine : 
- ` pickle with __getstate__ -> unpickle with __setstate__`
- ` direct pickle -> direct unpickle`

However my usecase is closer to : 
- `direct pickle -> unpickle with __setstate__`
I am actually reading json files ( That may have been created by hand, and should be as simple as possible, since they are exposed to end user ) and I want to build python objects from them ( while filtering, setting defaults, etc. ). Having to have the `"py/state"` level is a bit annoying.

I believe instead the type should be introspected to find if there is a `__setstate__()` method to be able to call it, even it `"py/state"` is not present.
Being able to work like this would make this `"py/state"` redundant, even when serializing with `__getstate__()`
This would enable these workflows as well : 
- ` direct pickle -> unpickle with __setstate__`
- ` pickle with __getstate__ -> direct unpickle`

So am I already out of date, using an obsolete version of jsonpickle, or am I correct in saying the support of `__getstate()__` and `__setstate__()` is not perfect ?
Or am I just trying to do something unintended with jsonpickle ?

issue_title: Enable overriding of retry_delay when running stuff manually
issue_body:
Currently if a task fails you need to wait 15 min (the default retry_delay) before the the central scheduler will let the same or another worker run the task. This is to prevent triggering too many tasks that are failing anyways. However, when runnings things manually this can be quite annoying and you can't always run with --local-scheduler (if you for example have multiple overlapping pipelines). 

I suggest adding a --force-retry flag or similar that allows you to override the retry_delay from the client side (but it should only override failed retry, not run the task if some other worker is currently running it, which is already possible through --central-scheduler).

Additionally, I suggest that tasks that are killed by user interrupts (KeyboardInterrupt) should send some kind of message to the scheduler when shutting down, notifying it to not set a retry_delay, since it's probably undesirable to have aborted manual runs prevent scheduled ones.

issue_title: "logger" isn't an actual requirement
issue_body:
Went to install the CloudFlare python library and was surprised it was [pulling in a module for logging](https://github.com/cloudflare/python-cloudflare/blob/5358208360c891cdabb954ba85c8ea269891a350/setup.py#L35) called `logger`. Looked through the CF code and as best as i can tell it's not importing `logger` anywhere, which is good b/c that module as a single .py and config file with the following which would mess up the logging config for most anything utilizing logging.

```
import logging
import logging.config
import os
import sys
import types

logging.getLogger('paramiko').setLevel(logging.WARNING)
logging.getLogger('requests').setLevel(logging.WARNING)

config_file = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'logging.conf')

logging.config.fileConfig(config_file, disable_existing_loggers=False)
logger = logging.getLogger()


def error(self, msg, *args, **kwargs):
    self.error(msg, *args, **kwargs)
    sys.exit(1)

logger.interrupt = types.MethodType(error, logger)
logger.info = logger.info
logger.warn = logger.warn
``` 

Pretty sure that line should just be dropped from the requirements list since everything being used is part of the stdlib.

issue_title: Transparent decompression of downloaded objects with gzip Content-Encoding
issue_body:
**Problem**: Most modern web browsers, curl (`curl --compressed "http://..."`), wget (`wget -S --header="accept-encoding: gzip" wordpress.com "http://..."`), and other tools will accept gzip-encoded web data and transparently decompress it before it is used or written out. boto3 has no built-in functionality to do this.

**Proposed solution**: It would be helpful if boto3's `download_file()` could transparently decompress any object stored with `Content-Encoding: gzip` metadata. This would obviate the need for downstream tools to navigate decompression with streams, etc.

If not the default, perhaps an option could be passed to`ExtraArgs` to enable transparent gzip decompression. Having an option would be similar to a capable browser informing a server to send gzip via a header of `Accept-Encoding: gzip`.  Since S3 cannot set the encoding based on the `Accept-Encoding` header sent, it would be helpful if boto3 could better handle common `Content-Encoding` types. The `gzip` type seems like a reasonable place to start, but perhaps `download_file()` could accept an encoding handler as a kwarg to address various other content types. 

Along with transparent decompression during download, it would be helpful to also specify an encoding handler for `upload_file()` that would act prior to upload and set the `ExtraArg` `ContentEncoding` accordingly.

issue_title: what we talk about when we talk about _files
issue_body:
The contents of the `_files` field for Literature record is supposed to contain the metadata to retrieve the file by `invenio-records-files`.

The schema we have for it was copied by Zenodo and so contains the basic info in the [invenio-records-files schema](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/master/invenio_records_files/jsonschemas/records-files/records-files-v1.0.0.json), but also some additional Zenodo-specific stuff (`previewer`, `type`) that we probably don't need.

The workflow is using this field in yet another way, writing `description` and `doctype` there (for arXiv PDF and extracted plots), which are not currently in the schema. This doesn't cause any error now as the results of `_files` are discarded anyway and never sent to legacy, but we should decide on what information we really want to have there. 

@kaplun and @tsgit know how files ares handled on legacy and could share their experience.
Discussing with @jacquerie, we identified the following keys that might be useful:

* `doctype` (or `document_type`?): to signal what kind of document is attached. This would be an `enum` with values `fulltext`, `plot`, **what else**?
* `mime_type`: how this document is encoded, which might warrant a different handling (e.g. PDF vs XML for a fulltext).
* `hidden`: a flag to indicate whether this file is publicly visible (would be true for fulltexts used for indexing that we may not serve directly to our users).

issue_title: Incorrect Error Handling in Django
issue_body:
The code in `contrib/django/middleware.py` has an issues related to how django handles errors:

When a view throws an exception, `TraceExceptionMiddleware.process_exception` gets called and  will set the `span.error = 1`. This is an issue, because other middleware might handle the exception and turn it into a non-500 response (e.g. `raise Http404` will be handled by django and turned into a 404 response, which should have `span.error = 0`).

## Potential Fix

One way to fix this would be to set `span.error` in `process_response` depending on the response's status code. For example:

     def process_response(self, request, response):
        ...
        span.error = 0 if response.status_code < 500 else 1
        span.finish()
        ...

This issue is related to a similar issue which has been reported and fixed for flask in `0.10.1` (see [Issue 390](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/issues/390))

issue_title: Environment values passed to Repo.clone_from() are not kept in the resulting repo

r = Repo.clone_from("git@github.com:bpiotr/whatever_repo.git", "/tmp/repo", env={"key": "value"})
    r.git.environment()
    {}

This is not what I expected when I called `Repo.clone_from` and documentation does not elaborate on the transient nature of the `env` here so IMHO this is a bug. 

BTW it costed me quite a while to figure out that `clone_from` actually uses `env` but any subsequent operations on the result needs it to have it set again.
